title: Angular Services and Dependency Injection
summary: Creating services with @Injectable, understanding hierarchical injectors, providedIn scopes, and injecting dependencies throughout an Angular app.

questions.prompt: What does `providedIn: 'root'` mean on an @Injectable service?
choices: The service is only available in the root component
A single instance is created and shared across the entire application (root injector). It's also tree-shakeable — not bundled if never injected
The service must be declared in the root module
The service is created fresh for every component
questions.explanation: `providedIn: 'root'` registers the service with the root injector. Angular creates exactly one instance, shared everywhere. The service is also tree-shaken out of the bundle if no component ever uses it — unlike adding to module providers[] which always includes it.

questions.prompt: You add `providers: [UserService]` in BOTH your AppModule and a specific component. What happens?
choices: An error — duplicate providers are not allowed
The component and its children get their OWN UserService instance, separate from the AppModule singleton
The component uses the AppModule instance
The component-level provider is ignored — module provider wins
questions.explanation: Angular's hierarchical injector: each entry in a component's `providers` creates a NEW instance for that component subtree.  The root singleton still exists for other components. This scoping is powerful but needs careful management — you may get subtle bugs if you expect shared state (singleton) but accidentally create a new instance.

questions.prompt: How many CartService instances exist when two separate <app-cart> components render?
choices: One — CartService is a singleton
Two — each CartComponent gets its own CartService instance
Zero — CartService needs to be in AppModule
Error — duplicate component instances
questions.explanation: When a service is in a component's `providers`, Angular creates a new instance for EACH component instance. Two `<app-cart>` elements → two separate CartService instances with independent state. This is intentional for isolated per-instance services like shopping carts, forms, wizard steps.

questions.prompt: What is the advantage of the `inject()` function over constructor injection?
choices: inject() is faster at runtime
inject() works anywhere during the injection context — in field initializers, factory functions, standalone injection, and can be used outside the constructor declaration
inject() replaces the need for @Injectable
inject() automatically handles async dependencies
questions.explanation: `inject()` can be called in field initializers (`private service = inject(MyService)`), in factory providers, in `rxjs` operators setup, and in functional interceptors/guards. Constructor injection requires listing all dependencies in the constructor signature. `inject()` removes boilerplate and enables injection in contexts where constructors aren't available.

questions.prompt: What is an `InjectionToken` used for?
choices: To authenticate API requests
To create a unique DI token for values that don't have a class type (strings, configs, objects, interfaces)
To track which services have been injected
It's an alias for @Injectable
questions.explanation: Angular's DI normally uses class types as keys. For non-class values (config objects, strings, interfaces), you create an `InjectionToken`. This gives the DI system a unique, type-safe key. Example: `new InjectionToken<AppConfig>('config')` then provide with `useValue`.

questions.prompt: What does `{ provide: UserService, useClass: MockUserService }` do?
choices: Merges the two services
When UserService is injected, Angular creates a MockUserService instance instead — useful for testing or providing alternate implementations
Extends UserService with MockUserService
Creates two separate services
questions.explanation: `useClass` substitutes one class for another. When any component injects `UserService`, Angular creates `MockUserService` instead. The injected type is still `UserService` (TypeScript type) but the runtime instance is `MockUserService`. Critical for unit testing without live HTTP calls.

questions.prompt: Angular resolves injected services by walking UP the injector tree. What happens if a service is NOT found anywhere in the tree?
choices: Returns undefined
Returns null
Throws a NullInjectorError at runtime (or compile-time if strict)
Creates a new instance automatically
questions.explanation: If Angular reaches the root injector without finding a provider, it throws `NullInjectorError: No provider for XService!`. Prevent this by: ensuring the service has `providedIn: 'root'`, or adding it to the right `providers` array, or using `@Optional()` if the service is truly optional.

questions.prompt: What does `@Optional()` on an injected dependency do?
choices: Makes the service lazy-loaded
Tells Angular: if this service can't be found, inject null instead of throwing NullInjectorError
Marks the service as optional in the providedIn configuration
Delays injection until first use
questions.explanation: `@Optional()` changes the missing-provider behavior from error to `null`. Used for truly optional features: `constructor(@Optional() private analytics?: AnalyticsService)`. You then null-check before use: `this.analytics?.track(event)`. Without @Optional, the NullInjectorError crashes the app.

questions.prompt: Which provider recipes are valid in Angular's `providers` array?
choices: { provide: Token, useValue: 'hello' }
{ provide: OldService, useExisting: NewService }
{ provide: Service, useAsync: asyncFactory }
{ provide: IService, useFactory: () => new IService() }
questions.explanation: `useValue`, `useExisting`, `useClass`, and `useFactory` are the four valid provider recipes. `useAsync` does not exist in Angular's DI — use `useFactory` with an async setup pattern instead.

questions.prompt: Why should you avoid `new MyService()` in a component to create service instances?
choices: Angular prevents direct `new` calls for decorated classes
It creates a new instance that's outside the DI system — no ability to swap for mocks in tests, no access to Angular's injected dependencies, and breaks the singleton guarantee
new is slower than DI
It's only a convention — there's no functional difference
questions.explanation: Creating services with `new` bypasses DI completely: (1) You can't inject other services into it (constructor would need explicit arguments). (2) Tests can't override it with mocks. (3) Multiple instances break any shared state. DI's entire purpose is to manage object lifecycle and enable substitutability.

questions.prompt: Where should you inject `HttpClient` when building an API service?
choices: Directly in each component that needs HTTP
In a dedicated service class — components inject the service, not HttpClient directly
In AppModule only
HttpClient doesn't need to be injected
questions.explanation: Best practice: create a service layer (UserService, ProductService) that wraps HttpClient calls. Components inject the service, not HttpClient directly. Benefits: (1) Components don't know about HTTP internals. (2) Easy to mock in tests. (3) Centralizes error handling and caching logic.

questions.prompt: `@Self()` and `@SkipSelf()` on an injected dependency both affect:
choices: Whether the service is cached
Which injector level Angular searches — @Self only searches the current component's injector; @SkipSelf skips the current and starts from the parent
The service's singleton lifetime
Whether the service can be inherited
questions.explanation: `@Self()` restricts injection lookup to only the current component's injector (not parents). Throws NullInjectorError if not found there. `@SkipSelf()` skips the current component's providers and looks only at ancestor injectors. Used for parent-child component communication patterns.

questions.prompt: This service uses `providedIn: 'root'` but is never imported or injected anywhere. What happens at bundle size?
choices: Still included — providedIn forces inclusion
Tree-shaken out — not included in the bundle since nothing injects it
Causes a build error
Included as a lazy chunk
questions.explanation: `providedIn: 'root'` services are tree-shakeable — if nothing ever injects `UnusedService`, the bundler (webpack/esbuild) omits it. This is a key advantage over `providers: [UnusedService]` in NgModule (always included regardless of usage). Always prefer `providedIn: 'root'` for this reason.

questions.prompt: What is a circular dependency between services and how do you fix it?
choices: ServiceA and ServiceB both extend a base class
ServiceA injects ServiceB, and ServiceB injects ServiceA — Angular can't construct either. Fix: extract shared logic into a third ServiceC
Two services with the same name in different modules
A service that injects itself
questions.explanation: Circular DI: Angular tries to create ServiceA → needs ServiceB → needs ServiceA → infinite loop. Angular detects this and throws a circular dependency error. Solution: find the shared functionality both need and extract it into a third service that neither depends on each other.

questions.prompt: Which are valid use cases for component-level providers (in @Component's providers array)?
choices: Form wizard where each step needs isolated form state service
Shopping cart component that should have its own CartService
A global auth service shared by all components
A dialog/modal that needs its own state service
questions.explanation: Component-level providers create isolated instances — good for per-component state. Auth services should be singletons at root level (shared state). Wizard steps, isolated carts, and dialogs with their own state are perfect use cases for component-level scoping.

questions.prompt: In a standalone Angular application (no NgModule), how do you provide HttpClient?
choices: Add HttpClientModule to AppModule
Add `provideHttpClient()` to the `providers` array in bootstrapApplication()
HttpClient is provided automatically in standalone apps
Inject HttpClient directly in each component
questions.explanation: In standalone apps (`bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, { providers: [...] })`), add `provideHttpClient()` (from `@angular/common/http`) to the providers array. This replaces `HttpClientModule`. The functional provider approach (`provideRouter`, `provideHttpClient`) is the modern Angular pattern.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of `useExisting` in a provider?
choices: Use an existing instance from another module
Create an alias: when A is injected, return the same instance as B (not a new instance). Different from useClass which creates a new instance
Check if a service already exists before creating one
Extend an existing service with new methods
questions.explanation: `useExisting` creates an alias — both tokens point to the SAME instance. `{ provide: OldTokenA, useExisting: NewTokenB }`: injecting OldTokenA gives you the exact same object as injecting NewTokenB. Contrast with `useClass`: that creates a NEW instance of the specified class.

questions.prompt: Why is DI essential for unit testing Angular services?
choices: It isn't — direct instantiation works fine for tests
Because DI lets you substitute real dependencies (HttpClient, Database) with mocks/stubs in TestBed, so tests run fast and deterministically without network/IO
DI is required for Angular test runner to discover test files
Only for integration tests — unit tests don't use DI
questions.explanation: In TestBed, you override providers: `{ provide: HttpClient, useClass: MockHttpClient }`. The service under test still uses its constructor (`private http: HttpClient`) but gets your mock. Without DI, you'd have to modify the service source code to swap implementations — violating separation of concerns.

questions.prompt: What does `@Self()` on a constructor parameter enforce?
choices: The service must be a singleton
Angular only looks in the current component's own injector — if not found there, throws NullInjectorError (even if the service exists higher up)
The service is self-initializing
Only native Angular services can be injected
questions.explanation: `@Self()` enforces locality — prevents accidentally inheriting parent services. Useful when a component must have its OWN provider of that service and not share a parent's. Combine with `@Optional() @Self()` to check if the component is configured correctly without crashing.

questions.prompt: A lazy-loaded route module provides a service. What is true about that service?
choices: It's the same singleton as the root-level service
It creates a new injector for the lazy module — the service is a separate instance from root, only available within that lazy module's component tree
Lazy modules cannot provide services
The service loads immediately at app start regardless
questions.explanation: Lazy-loaded modules get their own child injector. Services provided in a lazy module are separate instances from root. This can cause 'two instances' bugs if a service is in BOTH the root and lazy module's providers. Prefer `providedIn: 'root'` for shared services to avoid this.

flashcards.back: root: one singleton app-wide, tree-shakeable. Component providers[]: fresh instance per component instance (isolated state). Use root for shared services, component providers for isolated per-component state.

`private svc = inject(MyService)` — works in field initializers, factory functions, standalone. Constructor injection: `constructor(private svc: MyService)`. inject() is more flexible and preferred in modern Angular.
DI token for non-class values (configs, strings, interfaces). `new InjectionToken<AppConfig>('app.config')`. Provide with useValue/useFactory, inject with `inject(APP_CONFIG)`.
useClass: different implementation. useValue: constant. useFactory: computed value. useExisting: alias (same instance). Only useClass creates a new instance; useExisting reuses.
Injects null instead of throwing NullInjectorError if service not found. Combine with ?. operator: `this.analytics?.track()`. Use for optional features like analytics, A/B testing.
@Self: only search current component injector. @SkipSelf: skip current, start from parent. Use @Self to enforce a service is locally provided; @SkipSelf to access a parent version of a service.
Walks UP the injector tree from the requesting component. Current component → parent → grandparent → Module → Root. First found wins. @Self stops at current. @SkipSelf skips current.

`providedIn: 'root'` is tree-shakeable — excluded from bundle if never injected. `providers: [MyService]` in NgModule always included. Always prefer providedIn for services not needed in every build configuration.

project.brief: Design the dependency injection architecture for a feature-rich e-commerce app with these services: (1) AuthService — singleton, shares current-user state; (2) CartService — scoped to CartComponent, each cart instance has its own state; (3) ProductCacheService — singleton with in-memory cache; (4) AppConfig — an object with apiUrl and featureFlags, not a class. Decide: what `providedIn` or `providers` setting each gets, which provider recipe (useValue, useClass, useFactory) you'd use, and how you'd mock AuthService in a unit test. Bonus: explain what breaks if CartService accidentally ends up in the root providers.

checklist.text: CartService: providers: [CartService] in CartComponent — new instance per cart
AppConfig: InjectionToken with useValue or useFactory in root providers
Testing: TestBed.configureTestingModule({ providers: [{ provide: AuthService, useClass: MockAuthService }] })

hints: CartService in root providers: all CartComponents share ONE cart with one state — checkout from one cart empties another.
AppConfig: `export const APP_CONFIG = new InjectionToken<AppConfig>('config')` then `{ provide: APP_CONFIG, useValue: environment }` in providers.
Mocking AuthService: implement an interface or extend the class, override key methods to return test data without HTTP.
ProductCacheService providedIn root: same cache across all product-listing pages — fetching once populates for all.
